summary: Spolsky corrects his earlier analysis about Microsoft's open protocol specifications, acknowledging that readers pointed out existing open source projects integrating with Remote Desktop. He reflects on the problem of hasty news analysis, arguing that the original story tried to draw sweeping conclusions from a tiny fraction of the published protocols. He compares the complexity of the situation to the famous Schleswig-Holstein question, suggesting that snap analysis of major technology announcements is inherently unreliable.
key_points: Hasty news analysis that appears within hours of a breaking story is rarely good quality
The Microsoft protocol publication was far too complex to reduce to a single narrative about Remote Desktop
The real problem wasn't factual inaccuracy but rather the story's overambitious scope — drawing broad conclusions from a tiny data point
Complex technology policy questions resist quick summarization, much like intractable diplomatic disputes
money_quotes: I guess news analysis that appears within hours of a story breaking is never very good.
This is just one tiny fraction of the protocols that were published by Microsoft yesterday.
Charny's story was the moral equivalent of trying to decide who is going to win the next American election by talking to a couple of truckers at a bar.
The problem wasn't the accuracy in reporting (although that was sorely lacking), the problem was that the story was too ambitious.

The Schleswig-Holstein question is so complicated, only three men in Europe have ever understood it. One was Prince Albert, who is dead. The second was a German professor who became mad. I am the third and I have forgotten all about it.
key_insight: Snap news analysis of complex technology announcements inevitably oversimplifies, and the wisest response to being asked for instant commentary on genuinely complicated matters is to admit the complexity rather than pretend to have answers.
